title: St. Shenouda the Archimandrite, St. Elizabeth the Wonderworker & St. Sabas the Goth
narrative:
St. Shenouda the Archimandrite of White Monastery, St. Elizabeth the Wonderworker, and St. Sabas the Goth

On the twenty-fourth of Miazia, in the illuminated wake of the great feast of Saint George, the holy Church gathers three witnesses whose lives embody the three primary instruments through which the Paschal victory is communicated: the monastic community that keeps the liturgy alive, the wonder-worker through whom the Resurrection's healing power flows, and the soldier-martyr who brought the Georgian frontier into the universal Church. Saint Shenouda the Archimandrite — Shenoute of Atripe, the great White Monastery abbot — returns in the Miazia illumination with all the Paschal weight that his fierce, extraordinary life can carry. If the Lenten months portrayed Shenoute primarily as the legislator of asceticism and the defender of the poor, Miazia portrays him as the Paschal abbot: the man who presided at the great Easter vigil in a monastery of hundreds, perhaps thousands, of monks and nuns, who sang the Alleluia that ends the fast with a communal voice that the tradition imagines shaking the walls of the White Monastery. The feast of the Resurrection was, for Shenoute, the annual vindication of everything the fast had been — the proof that the emptying produced the filling, that the death produced the life, that the forty days of darkness produced the single morning of light.

Saint Elizabeth the Wonderworker is this day's discovery: a Byzantine abbess, venerated in the Eastern church as a wonder-worker of remarkable gifts, whose monastery became one of the great healing centres of Constantinople. She is occasionally confused with Elizabeth of Heraclea, but the tradition preserves her as a distinct figure whose ministry of healing encompassed not only physical ailments but the spiritual desolation that was the most common complaint of those who came to her monastery: the people who had lost their faith, or their hope, or their capacity for joy, and who found in Elizabeth's presence something that restored all three. In the Miazia illumination, Elizabeth the Wonderworker is the image of the Resurrection's most intimate application: the healing not of the body alone but of the interior life, the restoration of the person to the fullness of life that the Resurrection has made available.

Saint Sabas the Goth makes his second appearance in Miazia — already commemorated in Megabit 16 — because Basil the Great's letter in honour of his martyrdom is among the most theologically eloquent responses to the question of why a Gothic soldier drowned in a Wallachian river in three hundred and seventy-two should be commemorated by the Bishop of Caesarea and celebrated annually by the Ethiopian Church twelve centuries later. Basil's answer is the Miazia answer: that the martyrs belong to the universal Church, not to the local community that produced them, and that the blood shed in the Gothic forests is as precious to the Body of Christ as the blood shed in the arenas of Rome. Shenoute's Alleluia, Elizabeth's healing tears, Sabas's Wallachian drowning: three modes of the one Paschal victory that respects no border, speaks no single language, and is given entire to every person and every people who receive it. Amen.
